title: Trimming a text block by hand
subtitle: Using a finishing press and plough cutter to produce a clean, even fore-edge
excerpt: Trimming the fore-edge and sometimes the head and tail of a text block by hand produces the flush, smooth edges of a well-made bound book. The tool is the plough cutter, worked along the blade of a finishing press. The motion is slow and controlled, not a single sweeping cut.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on trimming edges. Internet Archive scan available. Cockerell describes the plough cutter and cutting press method in detail.

glossaryTerms.definition: A sliding cutting jig that rides along the cheeks of a finishing press to trim the edge of a clamped text block with a blade.
The process of cutting the open edge of a text block flush and even, removing any uneven page edges produced by folding.
A wooden or metal screw press used in bookbinding to hold a text block or book board firmly while edge-trimming, endbanding, or finishing operations are performed.

content.text: Trimming the text block with a
plough cutter
in a
finishing press

is one of the most satisfying operations in hand bookbinding. The plough advances in small increments across the edge of the block, removing a shaving of paper with each pass. Done well, the result is a flush, even
fore-edge trimming
that reveals all the signatures as a clean flat surface.
What you need
Finishing press: any wooden screw press with flat parallel cheeks. The text block must sit below the cheeks by the depth of the plough blade.
Plough cutter: check the blade is sharp before starting. A dull blade compresses and tears the paper instead of cutting it cleanly.
Setting up the press
Clamp the text block in the finishing press, spine at the bottom, fore-edge proud of the press cheeks by about 5 mm.
Check that the text block is square in the press: the fore-edge should be parallel to the cheek faces on both sides.
Mount the plough cutter with the blade set to make the first pass. Advance the blade by half a turn of the depth screw between passes; do not try to remove all the waste in one pass.
Ploughing the edge

Place the plough on the press cheeks with the blade resting against the text block edge. Push the plough away from you in a steady, even stroke, applying downward pressure. The blade will remove a thin shaving.
Advance the blade screw slightly. Make another pass in the same direction.
Continue advancing and ploughing until the edge is flush. Never return the plough toward you in contact with the block; always lift and return.
When the edge is clean, remove the block from the press and inspect. The signatures should show as even horizontal bands across the face.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: Blade tears the top pages while cutting cleanly below
attrs.cause: The blade is slightly tilted, cutting at an angle rather than flat to the edge.

content.text: Re-seat the plough on the press cheeks, check that both sides of the plough rest flat, and adjust the blade angle before continuing.

attrs.symptom: Shavings are compressed rather than curling away cleanly

content.text: Replace or sharpen the plough blade. A sharp blade produces a clear curling shaving; a blunt blade drags and compresses.
Variations

Trimming the head is done in the same way, with the text block rotated in the press so the head is proud. Trimming the tail after casing in is possible but harder because the spine and boards get in the way; trim tail and head before casing in if possible.
